text: The Place You Come from — I Was Born There
The Place You Come from — I Was Born There
"The place you come from — I was born there
The place you come from — I was born there
Tell me where you were born,
And I'll tell you where I was born.
You're from Lubya, I'm from Ijumbi.
Cow-sisters' children breed well together.
Tell me where you were born,
And I'll tell you where I was born.
I was born in Katoma of kinshwi,
You were born in Bulembo of yojwe.
Tell me where you were born,
And I'll tell you where I was born.
You're a child of the Ikanoni river,
I'm a child of the cattle roads,
The cattle roads to Itongo,
Ane others to Ilogelo.
I chopped a tree down in Ngalama, they say,
And the branches hit a house,
And twigs hit a house.
Stand still there, let me beguile you," he said.
"Stand still there, let me beguile you well.
You Ma Kilondo of the Mountain.
This is the greeting I would give you: 'Sleep well?'
They'll think we have one father."
"That little switch of twigs of yours," she said,
"That little switch of twigs in fours,
Whom did you cut that to hit?"
"I cut it to hit you!"
"Hitting, punishing me so! For what?"
"I punish you for your mistakes," he said,
I'm punishing you for your mistakes.
I asked you long ago to be blood brothers," he said.
"I asked you long ago to be blood brothers, you and I.
I asked you long ago to be blood brothers."
"Go stand at the River Ngono," he said,
"Go stand on the Ngono's bank,
And I'll stand on the other Ngono bank.
They'll think us trees that always grew there,
Muhumula trees found there long ago.
Again stand on the Ngono's bank,
And on the other Ngono bank I'll stand.
He said, "I'll fling it to you–catch it!
Dear one, it's you!"
Where he had met her,
Ma Kilondo of the Mountain.
"Rather than eat your mother's food, I'd eat you," he said.
"Those who eat you have acquired a taste.
Stop tormenting me with neighbor's closeness."
"Mother, Mother Dappled-cow, Oft-spoken-of," he said,
"Father who makes me well," she answered.
And speaking so, she consented.
The two hid her yams in a mushasha grove,
Near where Kilondo had been washing them.
The two hurried and entered,
Undressed and lay down.
He touched her on the thing Well-spoken-of,
When he began the folding,
When he entered inside, he found bananas ripe to falling from the stalk,
And higher up, the thick sweet mash kneading itself.
"Mother, Mother, Mother, Mother," he said,
"If you visit, if you visit," he said,
"If you find I'm cutting firewood,
The axe I'll throw above,
The hafted-knife I'll throw below.
We'll just walk together talking.
You Ma Kilondo of the Mountain,
Is this not desire? You possess me!
You possess me, you possess me!"
And if you visit," she said,
"If you visit, if you visit," she said,
If you find me peeling plantains,
Leave them. I'll wrap them up.
Let those who wanted them put them on the fire.
And if they leave them, let them burn."
"If you visit, if you visit," she continued,
"If you find me with the milk already poured,
And in the dairy room I'm churning,
Just tickle my ribs, I'll spill it out,
And let those who wanted it drink water.
Is this not desire? Sleep well?"
The two lay down and arose,
Went and dressed.
Went and found the yams again.
The woman Kilondo reached in her basket,
Took some nice kilibwa yams,
Placed them on a banana leaf,
Presented them to the man.
When at first he began to refuse them,
He stopped and thought, "I'll eat these yams.
I know 'The beautiful cannot be impure.'"
When he'd finished eating yams,
The man was then descending,
The woman was then climbing a hill.
He called to her again.
"Father Who-makes-me-well," she answered.
"Into your Broken-mouth-vessel," he said,
"I threw a clod of earth.
It burst in there.
When you bear a child," he said,
She wheeled around at once, Kilondo.
"You commoner's son, you've shamed me!
You've greatly shamed me! you've shamed me!
It's not a keg calabash for fermented brew.
Nor a tankard for sweet mash.
What they call a Broken-mouth-vessel'
Is the resonator for the enanga
That's the one that booms though it's been split."
"Mine is a thing Well-spoken-of," she declared.
"It's really a Broken-mouth-vessel," he said.
She turned around,
Saw her home was near,
Climbed a hill,
Passed two farms,
Reached her forecourt,
Entered the house,
And her harvest basket left; she put it in the inner room.
She went out again without delay,
Descended the hill,
Crossed a stream,
Met two men, and then she began:
"But that man truly shamed me!" she said.
"How did he cause you shame, Mother?" they asked.
"He met me on the path," she said.
He spoke of adult things and I agreed."
We came to lay down," she said,
And when we came to get up,
He called it a Broken-mouth-vessel.
I declare: It's not a keg calabash for fermented brew,
Nor a tankard for sweet mash.
What they call a Broken-mouth-vessel'
Is the resonator for the enanga
That's the one that booms though it's been split."
"Mine is a thing Well-spoken-of."
"Yes," they agreed, "It's a Broken-mouth-vessel."
What was once a secret broke open now.
She travelled to the lower court and made her accusation.
All the chairs creaked,
All officials judged,
"Yes," they agreed, "It's a Broken-mouth-vessel."
And a royal kinsman also judged,
"Yes," he agreed, "It's a Broken-mouth-vessel."
She stiffened her resolve; she went,
She travelled to the higher court and made her accusation.
All the chairs creaked,
And all the elders judged,
"Yes," they agreed, "It's a Broken-mouth-vessel."
And the Prime Minister also judged,
"Yes," he agreed, "It's a Broken-mouth-vessel."
She wept. Tears fell,
Ma Kilondo of the Mountain.
She was weeping there, when the king appeared.
"What makes you weep, woman?" he asked.
She wiped her tears.
"O King," she said, "What makes me weep this flood and bellow and wail,"
Once that man and I loved each other," she said.
"We were young then. We were children.
He's from Lubya,
I'm from Ijumbi.
He's from Katoma of kinshwi,
I'm from Bulembo of yojwe.
'When you grow up I'll marry you,' he'd say.
But when I grew up, he was not allowed to court me," she said.
"Father betrothed me somewhere else."
He met me just come out from bride's seclusion," she said. "I'd spent a year inside.
He spoke of adult things, and I agreed.
"We came to lay down, O King," she said,
"And when we came to get up,
It's not a keg calabash for fermented brew,
Nor a tankard for sweet mash.
What they call a Broken-mouth-vessel'
Is the resonator for the enanga
That's the one that booms though it's been split."
"O King," she said,"I was greatly angered.
I came to the lower court and made my accusation.
All the officials judged me,
'Yes,' they agreed 'It's a Broken-mouth-vessel.'
And the royal kinsman judged me too,
"I came here to the upper court," she said, "and made my accusation.
And all the elders judged me,
'Yes,' they agreed, 'It's a Broken-mouth-vessel.'
And even the Prime Minister judged me,
"And I was greatly angered," she said.
"And I shall wail, I shall bellow,
I shall fall into the lake of salt I weep.
Then they call it a Broken-mouth-vessel!"
The king inclined his head in thought; he raised it up.
"You truly win the case," he said.
"Father's going to court my mother," he said,
"When I was born they kissed me," he said,
"When father passed inside and I assumed the rule," he said, "they honored me.
They were honoring the thing Well-spoken-of."
"For when they demanded four cows in bridewealth,
They went. And those just born were carried with them.
And when they demanded a white sheep for the clan spirit," he said,
It also went, and with it nines of cowries."
It bears syphilis,
It bears gonorrhea,
It bears tertiary syphilis, whose pain cannot be spoken."
"How many judged you in the lower court?" he asked.
"Ten officials judged me," she said,
"And the royal kinsman was the eleventh."
"Let each official pay you ten," he said,
And let the royal kinsman pay fifteen.
Calling it a Broken-mouth-vessel shows
They know nothing of the folding."
"How many judged you here?" he asked.
"The elders numbered nine," she said. "The Prime Minister was the tenth."
"Let every elder pay fifteen," he said.
"Let the Prime Minister bring you twenty-five."
"You man! You!" he said,
"The one who called it a Broken-mouth-vessel,
Lie down. They'll thrash you five and twenty."
Down he lay. They thrashed him five and twenty.
He arose and grasped the king's knees for mercy.
"Stand again," he said, "Let me make it perfectly clear."
"So you'll know that you know nothing of the folding," he said,
"They'll boil kagigi beans for you
And sholosi beans that are black.
They'll cut Bellower enshankala plantains." he said,
And they'll cook you mole meat.
You know nothing of the folding."
A name he called–the husband who had followed after her.
He said, "You know the folding."
"They'll boil kamwami beans for you," he said,
"And kabanga beans that are white.
They'll find you meat that's rich," he said,
"My friend, I say rich kidney or the richest of the brisket."
"They'll cut you enshansha plantains." he said.
Eat the enshansha plantains,
Because you called it the thing Well-spoken-of,
Eat the enshansha plantains,
Eat them, feel happy in all your body."
What caused its great misfortune was–" he said,
"Its roar was diminished by its downward turn.
Had they suspended it
Say, high upon the mountain at Ilogelo,
And had they put a drumstick to it,
Its call would have been greater than the drum's."
